steps.title: Use the Zoque-only clue
steps.content: The passage says Mayan languages borrowed many words from Mixe-Zoquean during the period when the Olmecs dominated the region. The key detail is that all of those borrowed words come from the Zoque branch, not from the Mixe branch.

steps.title: Infer the timing
steps.content: If the Mixe-Zoquean family had not yet split when the borrowing occurred, the passage would not have a reason to identify the loanwords as specifically Zoque rather than generally Mixe-Zoquean. The Zoque-only pattern suggests the Zoque branch already existed as a distinct branch during Olmec dominance.

steps.title: Eliminate unsupported claims
steps.content: A adds borrowing into other Mixe-Zoquean languages, which the passage never discusses. B says Mixe languages supplanted Zoque languages, but the passage says present-day representatives of the family still include the Zoque branch. D wrongly treats Mayan as part of the same family, even though the passage says Mayan languages are otherwise unrelated. The answer is C.
